enrichment.summary: Responding to a viewer request from France, Kyle reflects on why internet communication is so poor — attributing it to the sudden democratization of voice (previously only educated/upper classes spoke publicly), the lag between technological invention and human readiness to use it, and people's lack of practice with two-way conversation. He also touches on ad hominem tactics, online trolling from his own experience, and ends with a brief addendum clarifying he wasn't trying to insult lower classes but explaining a historical skills gap.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: People aren't meaner — they're just unpracticed at public discourse; the internet gave a voice to the masses who never had to communicate sophisticatedly before
Attacking the person instead of the argument is dishonest and cheating, but it works because people are gullible enough to believe repeated claims
People stay anonymous because they fear losing arguments and appearing stupid, which exposes their insecurity
Humans take roughly 100 years to catch up to new inventions; the internet is no different — people aren't ready to use it maturely yet
Rather than being upset by widespread stupidity, treat it as an advantage — an opportunity to be smarter and create better content
Republicans are hypocritical — they oppose regulation on economics but over-regulate social issues like abortion
Believes in reincarnation and that souls evolve slowly, so modern humans are spiritually similar to people 500 years ago despite technological change
Upper classes historically had to develop diplomatic communication skills through necessity; lower classes didn't need to and so didn't develop them — not a matter of intelligence but practice
